subject: Chemistry
q: While titration dilute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCI</mi></math> solution with aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math>, which of the following will not be required ?
question: While titration dilute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCI</mi></math> solution with aqueous <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math>, which of the following will not be required ?
solution:
In this acid base Titrating there is no use of Bunsen burner and measuring cylinder other laboratory equipments will be required for getting the end point of titration.

These are the equipment required for titration.

  • Burette
  • Conical flask
  • Beaker
  • Pipette
  • Stirring rod
  • Small (filter) funnel
  • Burette stand and clamp (note 2)
  • White tile 
  • Indicator

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><msub><mi>MK</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub></math> solution is used for the titration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> solution in the acidic medium. The molarity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> solution is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math>
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><msub><mi>MK</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub></math> solution is used for the titration of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> solution in the acidic medium. The molarity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Fe</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> solution is____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math>
solution:
Equivalents of  K2Cr2O7= Equivalents of Fe2+

⇒(Molarity × volume × n.f) of K2Cr2O7= (molarity × volume × n.f) of Fe2+

n.f=n-factor(valence factor)

⇒0.02×20×6=M×10×1

⇒M=0.24

⇒ Molarity =24×10-2

subject: Chemistry
q:
In the following balanced reaction,
values of $X, Y$ and $Z$ respectively are
question:
In the following balanced reaction,
values of $X, Y$ and $Z$ respectively are
solution:
$X \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+Y \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-}+Z \mathrm{H}^{+} \leftrightharpoons$
$$
X \mathrm{Mn}^{++}+2 Y \mathrm{CO}_2+\frac{Z}{2} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$
First half reaction
$$
\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{++}
$$
On balancing
$$
\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+5 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{++}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$


Second half reaction
$$
\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2
$$
On balancing
$$
\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$
On multiplying eqn. (ii) by 5 and (iv) by 2 and then adding we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+5 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4^{--}+16 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \\
& 2 \mathrm{Mn}^{++}+10 \mathrm{CO}_2+8 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

question: Which of the following is a redox reaction ?
solution: The oxidation states show a change only in reaction (d)

subject: Chemistry
q:
2MnO4-+bC2O42-+cH+→xMn2++yCO2+zH2O
If the above equation is balanced with integer coefficients, the value of c is _______ . (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
question:
2MnO4-+bC2O42-+cH+→xMn2++yCO2+zH2O
If the above equation is balanced with integer coefficients, the value of c is _______ . (Round off to the Nearest Integer).
solution:
Writing the half reaction

Reduction half reaction 

MnO4-→Mn2+

balancing oxygen 

MnO4-→Mn2++4H2O

balancing Hydrogen 

8H++MnO4-→Mn2++4H2O

balancing charge 

5e-+8H++MnO4-→Mn2++4H2O

Oxidation half 

C2O42-→CO2
Balancing carbon

C2O42-→2CO2

Balancing charge

C2O42-→2CO2+2e-

Net equation

16H++2MnO4-+5C2O42-→10CO2+2Mn2++8H2O

So c=16

subject: Chemistry
q: The exact volumes of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> solution required to neutralise <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>PO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> solution and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>PO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> solution, respectively, are :
question: The exact volumes of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> solution required to neutralise <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>PO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> solution and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi>PO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> solution, respectively, are :
solution:
H3PO3+2NaOH→Na2HPO3+2H2O

50 ml1 M

1 M  V=?

⇒nNaoHnH3PO3=21

⇒1×V50×1=21⇒VNaOH=100 ml

H3PO2+NaOH→NaH2PO3+H2O

2M          1 M

100 mL       V=?

⇒nNaoHnH3PO3=11⇒1×V2×100=11⇒VNaOH=200 ml

subject: Chemistry
q: During the reaction of permanganate with thiosulphate, the change in oxidation of manganese occurs by value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>. Identify which of the below medium will favour the reaction.
question: During the reaction of permanganate with thiosulphate, the change in oxidation of manganese occurs by value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>. Identify which of the below medium will favour the reaction.
options: Both aqueous acidic and faintly alkaline
solution:
In neutral or weakly alkaline solution oxidation state of Mn changes by 3 unit. The reaction between permanganate and thiosulfate is a redox reaction, in which permanganate acts as an oxidizing agent and thiosulfate acts as a reducing agent.

MnO4⊝+S2O3-2→MnO2+SO4-2

This ionic mechanism is favoured in neutral aqueous medium.

subject: Chemistry
q: In order to oxidize a mixture of one mole of each of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> in acidic medium, the number of moles of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
question: In order to oxidize a mixture of one mole of each of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub><mo>)</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></math> in acidic medium, the number of moles of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
solution:
Eq.KMnO4⇒Eq.FeSO4+Eq.Fe2SO43+EqFeC2O4+Eq.Fe2C2O43
KMnO4⇒MnO4-
5e-+8H++MnO4-⇒Mn+2+4H2O V.F=5

So Eq.KMnO4⇒Eq.FeSO4+Eq.FeC2O4+Eq.Fe2C2O43
n×5=1×1+1×0+1×3+1×6
n×5=1+3+6
n=105=2 mole

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) $\mathrm{CH}_4(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \Rightarrow$ combination reaction
(B) 2 NaH (s) $\xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{s}) \quad+\mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \quad \Rightarrow$ Decomposition reaction
(C) $\mathrm{V}_2 \mathrm{O}_5(\mathrm{~s})+5 \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{s}) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{~V}(\mathrm{~s})+5 \mathrm{CaO}(\mathrm{s})$ $\Rightarrow$ Displacement reaction
(D) $2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ (aq.) $\xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ $\Rightarrow$ Disproportionation reaction

subject: Chemistry
q: The oxidation states of transition metal atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>FeO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> . The sum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> is______
question: The oxidation states of transition metal atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>Cr</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>7</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>KMnO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>FeO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively, are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> . The sum of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> is______
solution:
Compound  Oxidation state of transition element.

Oxidation states of K =+1 and O=-2

(i) K2Cr2O7     x=+6
(ii) KMnO4          y=+7
(iii) K2FeO4        z=+6
so (x+y+z)=19

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following cannot function as an oxidising agent?
question: Which of the following cannot function as an oxidising agent?
solution:
An oxidising agent is a substance that oxidises other substances involved in the reaction by gaining or accepting electrons from them.

So the lowest oxidation state substance will go to the higher oxidation state, which means it reduces others. Hence, it acts as a reducing agent. -3 is the lowest oxidation state for nitrogen. Hence, it only acts as a reducing agent.

Hence option A is the answer.

subject: Chemistry
q: An alkali is titrated against acid with methyl orange as an indicator, which of the following is a correct combination?
question: An alkali is titrated against acid with methyl orange as an indicator, which of the following is a correct combination?
options: <math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>

<math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>

<math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>

<math><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mtd><mtd><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">w</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mtd></mtr></mtable></math>
solution:
The pH range between 3.1 (red) and 4.4 (yellow) is the colour-change interval of methyl orange.

Weak base has pH > 7. So, when methyl orange indicator is added the solution turns yellow & if the solution is titrated against strong acid it turns pinkish red at the end point i.e., pH <3.1.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.5</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> oxalic acid is needed to neutralize <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mL</mi></math> of sodium hydroxide solution. What is the amount of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mL</mi></math> of the given sodium hydroxide solution?
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.5</mo><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> oxalic acid is needed to neutralize <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mL</mi></math> of sodium hydroxide solution. What is the amount of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>mL</mi></math> of the given sodium hydroxide solution?
solution:
millimoles of oxalic acid =50×0.5

milli equivalent of oxalic acid =50×0.5×2

millimoles of NaOH=25×M= milli equivalent of NaOH

∴50×0.5×2=25×M

⇒M=2

M=2 so, 1000 ml NaOH solution contains

=2 mol of NaOH

=2×40=80g NaOH

∴ 50ml NaOH solution contains =801000×50=4 g

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> equimolar mixture of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>yg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Nearest integer)
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> equimolar mixture of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>yg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>Na</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>.</mo></math> (Nearest integer)
solution:
Total mass=4g

Now

NaOH: a mol WNaOH+WNa2CO3=4

Na2CO3: 'a' mol ⇒40a+106a=4

⇒a=4146 mol

⇒ therefore mass of NaOH is: 4146×40 g

=1.095≈1

subject: Chemistry
options: Bromine is a stronger oxidant than iodine
Thiosulphate undergoes oxidation by bromine and reduction by iodine in these reaction
Bromine is a weaker oxidant than iodine
Bromine undergoes oxidation and iodine undergoes reduction in these reactions
solution:
In the reaction of $\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3{ }^{2-}$ with $\mathrm{I}_2$, oxidation state of sulphur changes to +2 to +2.5
In the reaction of $\mathrm{S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3{ }^{2-}$ with $\mathrm{Br}_2$, oxidation state of sulphur changes from +2 to +6 .
$\therefore$ Both $\mathrm{I}_2$ and $\mathrm{Br}_2$ are oxidant (oxidising agent) and $\mathrm{Br}_2$ is stronger oxidant than $\mathrm{I}_2$.

subject: Chemistry
q: Only \(2 \mathrm{~mL}\) of \(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\) solution of unknown molarity is required to reach the end point of a titration of \(20 \mathrm{~mL}\) of oxalic acid \((2 \mathrm{M})\) in acidic medium. The molarity of \(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\) solution should be \(\qquad\) M.
question: Only \(2 \mathrm{~mL}\) of \(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\) solution of unknown molarity is required to reach the end point of a titration of \(20 \mathrm{~mL}\) of oxalic acid \((2 \mathrm{M})\) in acidic medium. The molarity of \(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\) solution should be \(\qquad\) M.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { eq. }\left(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\right)=\text { eq. }\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right) \\ & \mathrm{M} \times 2 \times 5=2 \times 20 \times 2 \\ & \mathrm{M}=8 \mathrm{M}\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: KMnO4 is titrated with ferrous ammonium sulphate hexahydrate in presence of dilute H2SO4. Number of water molecules produced for 2 molecules of KMnO4 is _______
question: KMnO4 is titrated with ferrous ammonium sulphate hexahydrate in presence of dilute H2SO4. Number of water molecules produced for 2 molecules of KMnO4 is _______
solution:
By balancing redox reaction:

10[FeSO4.(NH4)2SO4.6H2O]+2KMNO4+8H2SO4→5Fe2(SO4)3+2MnSO4+10(NH4)2SO4+K2SO4+68H2O

8H++MnO4-+5Fe+2⟶5Fe+3+Mn+2+4H2O

For 2 moles of MnO4-, 8 moles of H2O. are released 5 moles of Mohr's salt contain 30 moles of H2O. So, 10 moles of Mohr's salt contain 60 moles of H2O So, total moles of H2O produced=68

subject: Chemistry
q: The redox reaction among the following is
question: The redox reaction among the following is
options: formation of ozone from atmospheric oxygen in the presence of sunlight
reaction of CoH2O6Cl3 with AgNO3

reaction of <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msub></math> with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>
combination of dinitrogen with dioxygen at <math><mn>2000</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>
solution:
(i)     N 2 + O 2 → 2000 K 2NO  (Re dox reaction)

during the reaction, oxidation of nitrogen take place from 0 to 2 and reduction of oxygen take place from 0 to –2. It means this reaction is redox reaction.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A) : An aqueous solution of KOH when for volumetric analysis, its concentration should be checked before the use.

Reason (R) : On aging,KOH solution absorbs atmospheric CO2.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A) : An aqueous solution of KOH when for volumetric analysis, its concentration should be checked before the use.

Reason (R) : On aging,KOH solution absorbs atmospheric CO2.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: (A) is not correct but (R) is correct
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
solution:
KOH absorb CO2 based on the following reaction:
CO2(g) + 2KOH(aq) → K2CO3(aq) + H2O(l)
Thus, concentration of Carbon dioxide decreases over time, prior to volumetric analysis concentration of potassium hydroxide should be checked.

subject: Chemistry
q: In the reaction of oxalate with permanganate in acidic medium, the number of electrons involved in producing one molecule of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is:
question: In the reaction of oxalate with permanganate in acidic medium, the number of electrons involved in producing one molecule of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is:
solution:
2 MnO4-+5C2O42-+16H+→10 CO2+2 Mn2++8H2O

C goes from +3 oxidation state to +4 oxidation state.

Total 10 e- are transferred for 10 molecules of CO2.

Hence, for 1 molecule of CO2, only one e- is transferred.
So, number of electrons involved for 1 molecule of CO2  is 1.

subject: Chemistry
q: One mole of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ was passed into 2 L of cold 2 M KOH solution. After the reaction, the concentrations of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{ClO}^{-}$and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$are respectively (assume volume remains constant)
question: One mole of $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ was passed into 2 L of cold 2 M KOH solution. After the reaction, the concentrations of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{ClO}^{-}$and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$are respectively (assume volume remains constant)
solution:
Cl₂ undergoes disproportionation in cold KOH: $Cl_2 + 2OH^- \rightarrow Cl^- + ClO^- + H_2O$
Initial: 1 mole Cl₂, 2 L × 2 M KOH = 4 moles OH⁻
Molar ratio Cl₂:OH⁻ = 1:2 (stoichiometric)
Products formed: 1 mole Cl⁻, 1 mole ClO⁻; Excess OH⁻ = 4 - 2 = 2 moles
In 2 L solution: [Cl⁻] = 0.5 M, [ClO⁻] = 0.5 M, [OH⁻] = 1 M

question: Identify the process in which change in the oxidation state is five :

(iv) 4.4 mL
(v) 4.4 mL
If the volume of oxalic acid taken was 10.0 mL then the molarity of the NaOH solution is ___M. (Rounded-off to the nearest integer)
question:
Consider titration of NaOH solution versus 1.25 M oxalic acid solution. At the end point following burette readings were obtained.
(i) 4.5 mL
(ii) 4.5 mL
(iii) 4.4 mL
(iv) 4.4 mL
(v) 4.4 mL
If the volume of oxalic acid taken was 10.0 mL then the molarity of the NaOH solution is ___M. (Rounded-off to the nearest integer)
solution:
VNaOH=4.4 ml

eq. of NaOH= eq. of H2C2O4

or, M×4.4×1=1.25×10×2

or, M=5.68M

∴ Nearest integer answer is 6.

subject: Chemistry
q: A compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' is a weak acid and it exhibits colour change at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> close to the equivalence point during neutralization of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></math>. Compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' exists in ionized form in basic medium. The compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' is
question: A compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' is a weak acid and it exhibits colour change at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>pH</mi></math> close to the equivalence point during neutralization of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi>COOH</mi></math>. Compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' exists in ionized form in basic medium. The compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' is
solution:
phenolphthalein, (C20H14O4), an organic compound of the phthalein family that is widely employed as an acid-base indicator. As an indicator of a solution's pH, phenolphthalein is colourless below pH 8.5 and attains a pink to deep red hue above pH 9.0.

It is used for the titration of weak acid and strong base. Phenolphthalein is weak acid give colour in basic medium. It exist in ionized form in basic medium.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given that,

EO2/H2Oo=+1.23 V;
ES2O82-/SO42-o=2.05 V
EBr2/Br-o=+1.09 V;
EAu3+/Auo=1.4 V


The strongest oxidizing agent is
question:
Given that,

EO2/H2Oo=+1.23 V;
ES2O82-/SO42-o=2.05 V
EBr2/Br-o=+1.09 V;
EAu3+/Auo=1.4 V


The strongest oxidizing agent is
solution:
For strongest oxidizing agent, the standard reduction potential should be highest.

An oxidizing agent, or oxidising agent is a substance that has the ability to oxidize other substances — in other words to accept their electrons. Common oxidizing agents are oxygen, hydrogen peroxide and the halogens.

subject: Chemistry
q: Excess of $\mathrm{KI}$ reacts with $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$ solution and then $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ solution is added to it. Which of the statements is incorrect for this reaction?
question: Excess of $\mathrm{KI}$ reacts with $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$ solution and then $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ solution is added to it. Which of the statements is incorrect for this reaction?
solution:
$2 \mathrm{CuSO}_4+4 \mathrm{KI}$ (excess) $\longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{I}_2+\mathrm{I}_2 \uparrow$
$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+\mathrm{I}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_4 \mathrm{O}_6+2 \mathrm{Nal}$

subject: Chemistry
q: An example of a disproportionation reaction is
question: An example of a disproportionation reaction is
options: <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>→</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math>
solution:
Disproportionation reaction: In this type of reaction, same elemental species gets oxidised as well as reduced.

 Na+1Br-1+Cl20→2Na+1Cl-1+Br20-not disproportionate,

2KMn+7O4→K2Mn+6O4+Mn+4O2+O2-not disproportionate,

2Mn+7O4-+10J-+16H+→2Mn+2+SI2+8H2O-not disproportionate
 

-It is disproportionate.

subject: Chemistry
q: The strength of an aqueous <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> solution is most accurately determined by titrating: (Note: consider that an appropriate indicator is used)
question: The strength of an aqueous <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> solution is most accurately determined by titrating: (Note: consider that an appropriate indicator is used)
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> in a pipette and aqueous oxalic acid in a burette

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> in a burette and aqueous oxalic acid in a conical flask
Aq.NaOH in a burette and concentrate H2SO4 in a conical flask
Aq.NaOH in a volumetric flask and concentrated H2SO4 in a conical flask
solution:
Oxalic acid is a primary standard solution while H2SO4 is a secondary standard solution.

Titration is a common laboratory method of quantitative chemical analysis to determine the concentration of an identified analyte. A reagent, termed the titrant or titrator, is prepared as a standard solution of known concentration and volume.
